HS-ESS1-1.official: Develop a model based on evidence to illustrate the life span of the sun and the role of nuclear fusion in the sun's core to release energy that eventually reaches Earth in the form of radiation.
HS-ESS1-1.plain: I can explain how the Sun makes its energy and how that energy reaches and warms Earth.
HS-ESS1-1.assessedAt: Goldilocks Report — framework criteria involving the host star (K/U strand, Component 1 locks U1 Block 7)
HS-ESS1-1.onTrack: Your habitability framework treats the host star as an energy source with numbers attached (luminosity, HZ range), not just 'the planet needs sunlight.'

HS-ESS1-2.official: Construct an explanation of the Big Bang theory based on astronomical evidence of light spectra, motion of distant galaxies, and composition of matter in the universe.
HS-ESS1-2.plain: I can explain what the evidence — starlight, moving galaxies, the makeup of the universe — tells us about how the universe began.
HS-ESS1-2.assessedAt: Previewed in U1 Block 4 (lookback time); formally assessed in a later unit
HS-ESS1-2.onTrack: You can explain why looking far away means looking back in time, and what that has to do with evidence for the universe's history.

HS-ESS1-4.official: Use mathematical or computational representations to predict the motion of orbiting objects in the solar system.
HS-ESS1-4.plain: I can use math and models to predict how planets and other objects orbit.
HS-ESS1-4.assessedAt: Goldilocks Report — transit/orbit reasoning in your exoplanet analysis (T/T strand, Component 4 locks U1 Block 9)
HS-ESS1-4.onTrack: You can read a light curve: dip spacing → orbital period, and you know what that math can and can't tell you.

HS-ESS2-1.official: Develop a model to illustrate how Earth's internal and surface processes operate at different spatial and temporal scales to form continental and ocean-floor features.
HS-ESS2-1.plain: I can explain how Earth's surface features are built and reshaped by processes working at very different speeds and sizes.
HS-ESS2-1.assessedAt: Landscape Reading — biography past + current processes (K/U strand, Components 1–2 lock U2 Block 6)
HS-ESS2-1.onTrack: Your location's biography names specific processes at specific timescales — 'monsoon pulses move the beach yearly; the harbour changed it in decades' — not just 'erosion happened.'

HS-ESS2-2.official: Analyze geoscience data to make the claim that one change to Earth's surface can create feedbacks that cause changes to other Earth systems.
HS-ESS2-2.plain: I can show how one change on Earth sets off feedback loops that change other Earth systems.
HS-ESS2-2.assessedAt: Goldilocks Report — framework criteria with feedback reasoning, esp. field 5 'where it can fail' (K/U, Component 1 locks U1 Block 7)
HS-ESS2-2.onTrack: You can run the perturbation test on a loop and say whether it amplifies or stabilizes — and your framework uses that move on real criteria.

HS-ESS2-5.official: Plan and conduct an investigation of the properties of water and its effects on Earth materials and surface processes.
HS-ESS2-5.plain: I can investigate how water shapes rock, sediment, and land.
HS-ESS2-5.assessedAt: Landscape Reading — river/coast process analysis for water-shaped locations (K/U + T/T, Components 1–4)
HS-ESS2-5.onTrack: You can point at a Chennai landform and name the specific water process responsible, with evidence from imagery or data.

HS-ESS3-1.official: Construct an explanation based on evidence for how the availability of natural resources, occurrence of natural hazards, and changes in climate have influenced human activity.
HS-ESS3-1.plain: I can explain, with evidence, how resources, hazards, and climate have shaped where and how people live.
HS-ESS3-1.assessedAt: Landscape Reading — projections + recommendations (T/T strand, Components 4–5 lock U2 Block 8)
HS-ESS3-1.onTrack: Your 50-year projections connect physical processes to human decisions — and your recommendation names who would act and why.

HS-ESS2-4.official: Use a model to describe how variations in the flow of energy into and out of Earth's systems result in changes in climate.
HS-ESS2-4.plain: I can use a model to show how energy in vs. energy out sets Earth's climate.
HS-ESS2-4.assessedAt: Chennai Climate Brief — the science sections (K/U components)
HS-ESS2-4.onTrack: Your brief's physics section traces an energy flow, not just names 'greenhouse effect.'

HS-ESS2-6.official: Develop a quantitative model to describe the cycling of carbon among the hydrosphere, atmosphere, geosphere, and biosphere.
HS-ESS2-6.plain: I can follow carbon through Earth's four spheres — with numbers.
HS-ESS2-6.onTrack: You can say where the ~10 GtC/year of human carbon goes, and why the one-way flux matters.

HS-ESS3-5.official: Analyze geoscience data and the results from global climate models to make an evidence-based forecast of the current rate of global or regional climate change and associated future impacts to Earth systems.
HS-ESS3-5.plain: I can use real data and model results to forecast climate change where I live — and say how confident to be.
HS-ESS3-5.assessedAt: Chennai Climate Brief — the regional-projection components (T/T)
HS-ESS3-5.onTrack: Your Chennai projections cite data AND name the model's uncertainty — forecast, not prophecy.

HS-ESS3-6.official: Use a computational representation to illustrate the relationships among Earth systems and how those relationships are being modified due to human activity.
HS-ESS3-6.plain: I can use models and data tools to show how human activity is changing the links between Earth's systems.
HS-ESS3-6.assessedAt: Chennai Climate Brief — adaptation analysis (T/T) + Policy Panel (C)
HS-ESS3-6.onTrack: Your recommendation connects a human decision to a system response, with a tool or dataset behind it.

HS-ESS1-5.official: Evaluate evidence of the past and current movements of continental and oceanic crust and the theory of plate tectonics to explain the ages of crustal rocks.
HS-ESS1-5.plain: I can use evidence — seafloor ages, GPS motion, fossil matches — to explain how plates move.
HS-ESS1-5.onTrack: Your hazard profile explains WHY your city's boundary behaves as it does, with evidence.

HS-ETS1-1.official: Analyze a major global challenge to specify qualitative and quantitative criteria and constraints for solutions that account for societal needs and wants.
HS-ETS1-1.plain: I can pin down what a solution must do — and what limits it — before I design.
HS-ETS1-1.assessedAt: City Hazard Profile → Engineering Portfolio scenario setup
HS-ETS1-1.onTrack: Your Hazard Profile names the criteria and constraints your design must satisfy.

HS-ETS1-2.official: Design a solution to a complex real-world problem by breaking it down into smaller, more manageable problems that can be solved through engineering.
HS-ETS1-2.plain: I can break a big engineering problem into pieces I can actually solve.
HS-ETS1-2.onTrack: Each iteration attacks a named sub-problem, and your Division-of-Labour Plan shows the breakdown.

HS-ETS1-3.official: Evaluate a solution to a complex real-world problem based on prioritized criteria and trade-offs that account for a range of constraints, including cost, safety, reliability, and aesthetics, as well as possible social, cultural, and environmental impacts.
HS-ETS1-3.plain: I can judge a design by weighing its trade-offs — cost, safety, who it protects, who it leaves out.
HS-ETS1-3.onTrack: Your defense names what your design sacrifices, not just what it achieves.

HS-ETS1-4.official: Use a computer simulation to model the impact of proposed solutions to a complex real-world problem with numerous criteria and constraints on interactions within and between systems relevant to the problem.
HS-ETS1-4.plain: I can use a simulation to test how my design choices play out before committing to them.
HS-ETS1-4.assessedAt: Engineering Design Portfolio — logged sim iterations (T/T)
HS-ETS1-4.onTrack: Each iteration is tested in the simulator and its outputs cited as evidence — changed, run, compared.

HS-ESS3-3.official: Create a computational simulation to illustrate the relationships among management of natural resources, the sustainability of human populations, and biodiversity.
HS-ESS3-3.plain: I can use a computational model to explore how resource choices affect people and places.
HS-ESS3-3.onTrack: Your sim budget decisions connect spending to who gets protected — and who doesn't.

SEP-4.plain: I can read real data — charts, imagery, light curves — and say what it does and doesn't show.
SEP-4.assessedAt: Runs through every unit; graded wherever data feeds a claim
SEP-4.onTrack: You cite the data behind your claims and name what the data can't tell you.

SEP-6.plain: I can build an explanation from evidence — not just state an answer.
SEP-6.assessedAt: Every summative's analysis sections (K/U + T/T strands)
SEP-6.onTrack: Your explanations name mechanisms ('because the core cooled…'), not just outcomes ('it's cold').

SEP-7.plain: I can defend a claim under questioning, cite my evidence, and revise when someone shows me better reasoning.
SEP-7.assessedAt: Every defense (C strand): U0 Block 7 onward
SEP-7.onTrack: In Q&A you use the five moves — and 'I don't know, here's what would settle it' when true.

SEP-8.plain: I can find sources, judge them with OPVL, and communicate what I learned honestly.
SEP-8.assessedAt: Source Dossier (all strands) + every OPVL component after it
SEP-8.onTrack: Your Limitations are specific to the source in front of you — never 'it might be biased.'

SEP-2.plain: I can build and use models — diagrams, simulations, cross-sections — to explain how something works.
SEP-2.assessedAt: Across units; in U3, the sim iterations and boundary cross-sections
SEP-2.onTrack: Your model shows the mechanism, not just the label — arrows mean something.

SEP-5.plain: I can use numbers, budgets, and computational tools to reason about a problem.
SEP-5.assessedAt: U3 sim iterations — score and budget reasoning in the engineering journal
SEP-5.onTrack: Your journal cites the numbers — scores, budget lines — and reasons from them.

Engage 3.official: Evaluate whether an AI output should be accepted, revised, or rejected (paraphrased — full framework at ailiteracyframework.org)
Engage 3.plain: I can check an AI's answer against real sources and decide: use it, fix it, or bin it.
Engage 3.assessedAt: Every AI Documentation Template — the 'one thing the AI got wrong' field (K/U gate)
Engage 3.onTrack: You catch specific errors ('it said 2024; the report says 2023') — and you've stopped writing 'AI can hallucinate.'

Engage 5.official: Understand how AI systems consume energy and natural resources (paraphrased)
Engage 5.plain: I can estimate what my AI use costs in electricity and water, and decide when it's worth it.
Engage 5.onTrack: You can put rough numbers on an AI choice and name one limitation of your own estimate.

Create 3.official: Direct generative AI to elicit feedback, refine work, and support reflection (paraphrased)
Create 3.plain: I can use AI to make MY work better — and show exactly who changed what.
Create 3.assessedAt: Revision Trail — practiced U1/U2 Block 8, assessed with the U5 Position Paper
Create 3.onTrack: Your trail has modified and rejected rows, not just accepted ones — the judgment stays yours.

Manage 1.official: Decide whether AI is the right tool for a task before using it (paraphrased)
Manage 1.plain: I choose when to use AI and when to think it through myself — and I can defend either choice.
Manage 1.assessedAt: AI-use log across the year; the defended not-using-AI reflection
Manage 1.onTrack: You can name a step where you chose NOT to use AI, and your reason survives a 'why?'
